metadata.summary: Four-stage design-to-dev workflow for spatial XR: 2D wireframes → ShapesXR low-fi → high-fi Unity/Unreal → production with accessibility.

content.title: Spatial Design Tools
content.summary: Tool selection guide and design-to-development workflow for spatial (XR) design. Covers the gap between 2D design tools (Figma) and native spatial tools (ShapesXR, Gravity Sketch), and defines a four-stage workflow from concept through production: 2D wireframes → low-fi headset prototype → high-fi Unity/Unreal → production.

why.p: Spatial design cannot be adequately specified or tested in traditional 2D tools. Key interaction qualities (depth, scale, arm reach, comfort) are invisible in Figma mockups and only manifest in a headset. A staged workflow that introduces the headset at the low-fi prototype stage catches spatial UX problems before costly high-fidelity development.

list.item: Selecting tools for a new spatial/XR design project
Setting up a design-to-development handoff process for an XR team
Deciding when to move from 2D to 3D in the design process
Recommending tool investments to stakeholders
2D screen-based UI design — standard Figma/Sketch workflow applies
XR content production (3D modeling, animation) — separate tools and skills
Enterprise XR requirements specification — use enterprise-xr-applications instead
Figma with 3D plugins: 2D wireframes with spatial annotations; suitable for concept and specification only
ShapesXR: VR-native low-fidelity prototyping directly in a headset; primary tool for early spatial UX validation
Gravity Sketch: VR-native 3D design and sketching; suitable for 3D form design and organic spatial layouts
Unity: industry-standard XR development platform; used for high-fidelity interactive prototypes and production
Unreal Engine: high-fidelity rendering for XR; suited to visualization-heavy enterprise applications
Reality Composer Pro: Apple's tool for visionOS spatial content; required for Vision Pro production
Spark AR: Meta AR effects and passthrough content; consumer-focused AR

rule.statement: Stage 1 (Concept): Use 2D wireframes in Figma with explicit spatial annotations — depth layers, scale references relative to human height, reach zones. Do not attempt to design spatial interactions in 2D beyond basic flow.
rule.rationale: 2D tools cannot represent depth, scale, or arm reach; spatial annotations communicate intent to developers without requiring 3D tooling at concept stage.

rule.statement: Stage 2 (Low-fi Prototype): Build a rough spatial prototype in ShapesXR or equivalent and test it in the target headset with representative users before writing any production code.
rule.rationale: Key spatial UX problems — arm fatigue, uncomfortable viewing angles, depth misperception, scale errors — are invisible in 2D mockups and only manifest in the headset. Catching them at low-fi stage costs a fraction of fixing them post-development.

rule.statement: Stage 3 (High-fi Prototype): Implement in Unity or Unreal with real interactions, performance constraints, and target hardware. Conduct a second round of user testing in the target environment (factory floor, office, home).
rule.rationale: Performance constraints (frame rate, rendering budget) affect interaction feel; high-fi prototypes in the development engine expose these issues before production optimization.

rule.statement: Stage 4 (Production): Include an accessibility review — check gaze+dwell fallback for motor-impaired users, voice command support, comfort for long sessions, and color contrast for passthrough AR overlays.
rule.rationale: Spatial accessibility is rarely addressed in production without an explicit review gate; omitting it excludes motor-impaired users and may violate enterprise accessibility requirements.

rule.statement: Move to headset testing at Stage 2 (low-fi), not later. If the team lacks access to the target hardware, use the nearest available headset — any 6DOF device is more informative than any 2D mockup for spatial UX.
rule.rationale: Delaying headset testing to high-fi or production is the most common cause of late-stage XR UX failures; the headset test must be part of the design process, not a QA activity.

rule.statement: Use Claude as a "spatial pipeline librarian" — it picks the right tool per stage (concept → low-fi → high-fi → production), generates scaffolding (Unity C# MonoBehaviours, USDA scene files, glTF transforms), and writes the headset test plan. The headset-bound creative work (hand-sketching in ShapesXR, sculpting in Gravity Sketch) stays human; the agent prepares import scripts, file conventions, and review checklists around it. Always pair agent output with a human in-headset validation pass — pixel-perfect 2D ≠ comfortable 3D.
rule.rationale: Agentic workflow separates deterministic scaffolding from creative and embodied design work, allowing agents to accelerate the pipeline without replacing human spatial intuition.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Core tools and services for spatial design pipeline:

list.item: usdpython / usdcat: Convert / inspect USD scenes (https://openusd.org/release/install.html)
gltf-pipeline: glTF optimization, Draco compression (npm i -g gltf-pipeline)
gltf-validator: Validate glTF before headset import (npm i -g gltf-validator)
Unity -batchmode CLI: Headless Unity build for XR (https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/CommandLineArguments.html)
Unreal RunUAT.bat BuildCookRun: Headless Unreal XR packaging (https://docs.unrealengine.com/automation/)
xcrun reality-composer-pro: macOS Reality Composer Pro CLI (https://developer.apple.com/documentation/realitykit)

section.p: SaaS services: ShapesXR (SaaS, no public API), Gravity Sketch (limited Figma plugin bridge), Unity Cloud Build (REST API), Reality Composer Pro (macOS GUI-only), Adobe Aero (export-only).

list.item: Pin one tool per stage in the project README; agent should not switch mid-feature.
Scale all assets to real-world meters at export, never "design units".
Bake lighting in the design tool; runtime baking blows perf budgets on Quest 3 / Vision Pro.
Ship a xr-handoff/ folder with USDZ + glTF + screenshots; agents read the manifest, not the binaries.
Version 3D assets via Git LFS, not raw Git — diffs are useless and bloat the repo.
Test on actual hardware every sprint; emulator comfort never matches headset comfort.
Claude can hallucinate Unity APIs that exist only in older XR Toolkit versions — pin the target version in the prompt.
USDA/USDZ JSON-like syntax fools LLMs into emitting almost-valid files; always run usdchecker before import.
Token-cheap output: ask for scene graphs as JSON, not prose; saves 60–80% tokens vs. natural-language scene descriptions.
Human checkpoint required before headset upload — comfort, occlusion, and anchor drift cannot be predicted from text.
Don't let the agent author shaders; ShaderGraph / Material X visual tooling is the source of truth.
IP risk: Gravity Sketch / ShapesXR cloud may store proprietary models; agent should warn before any cloud upload.
